title: Getting Started
description: Your first steps with AI agents — from your first conversation to creating your own specialized agent.

modules.title: Meet Your AI
modules.agentInstructions: Start here. Make sure the learner understands what agents are conceptually before they start using them. Keep it conversational, not lecture-like.

steps.title: What is an agent?
steps.content:
An AI agent is more than a chatbot. It's a persistent assistant with its own memory, workspace, tools, and personality. On this platform, each agent has:

- **A system prompt (CLAUDE.md)** — instructions that define who it is and how it behaves
- **A workspace** — a folder or project it can read and modify
- **Tools** — abilities like reading files, writing code, running commands
- **Memory** — conversation history and learned facts that persist across sessions
- **MCP integrations** — connections to external services (Slack, GitHub, databases, etc.)

Think of agents like team members. Each one has a specialty, their own desk (workspace), and their own set of tools. You can have a coding agent, a research agent, a writing agent — each optimized for different work.
verificationQuestions: What's the difference between an agent and a regular chatbot?
Name two things that make each agent unique (hint: think about what they know and what they can do)

steps.title: Your first conversation
steps.content:
Time to actually talk to an agent. Go to the **Chat** page (/ui), pick any agent, and have a real conversation. Ask it to help you with something — don't just say 'hello'. Give it a task:

- Ask it to explain something you're curious about
- Ask it to draft an email or message
- Ask it to help you think through a problem

Send at least 5 messages back and forth. Get a feel for the rhythm of working with an agent.

modules.title: Give It Context
modules.agentInstructions: This module is about prompt quality. Emphasize the before/after difference — users should feel the improvement in output when they provide better input.

steps.title: What makes a good prompt?
steps.content:
The quality of what you get from AI is directly proportional to the quality of what you give it. Here's what makes a prompt effective:

**Context** — Tell it what you're working on and why. "I'm preparing a quarterly report for my board" is better than "write a report."

**Specifics** — Be precise about what you want. Format, length, tone, audience. "Write a 3-paragraph summary in plain English for non-technical stakeholders" beats "summarize this."

**Examples** — When possible, show it what good looks like. Paste in a previous version, a competitor's approach, or a template.

**Constraints** — Tell it what NOT to do. "Don't use jargon. Keep it under 200 words. Don't make up numbers."
verificationQuestions: What are the four elements of an effective prompt?
Why is 'write a report' a weak prompt? How would you improve it?

steps.title: Try a detailed prompt
steps.content:
Now put it into practice. Go to any agent and send a prompt that includes:

1. **Context** — what you're working on
2. **Specifics** — exactly what you want
3. **Constraints** — what to avoid

Compare the result to a vague version of the same request. Notice the difference.

steps.title: Upload a file to your agent
steps.content:
Agents can work with files you upload. Try it:

1. Go to any agent's chat
2. Click the 📎 attachment button
3. Upload a document, spreadsheet, image, or any file
4. Ask the agent to analyze, summarize, or work with the file

This is powerful — instead of copy-pasting content, you can hand the agent the actual file and let it work directly with it.

modules.title: Make It Yours
modules.agentInstructions: This is the capstone module. The learner should leave with a working specialized agent. Help them think about what's genuinely useful for their work, not just a toy example.

steps.title: Create a specialized agent
steps.content:
Now create an agent that's built for YOUR work. Go to the **Org** page (/org) and click **+ New Agent**. Think about:

- **What's one recurring task** you do that AI could help with?
- **What project** are you working on that needs a dedicated assistant?

Examples:
- A "Meeting Notes" agent that summarizes your meeting recordings
- A "Code Reviewer" agent pointed at your project repo
- A "Content Writer" agent with your brand guidelines in its system prompt

Create the agent, give it a clear name and description, and point its workspace at a relevant folder.

steps.title: Write a system prompt
steps.content:
Your new agent needs instructions. Its CLAUDE.md file is its personality and expertise. Go to the agent's dashboard and write a system prompt that tells it:

- **Who it is** — its role and expertise
- **What it should do** — its primary tasks
- **How it should behave** — tone, format preferences, constraints
- **What it knows** — domain context, key facts

A good system prompt is like onboarding a new team member. The more context you give, the better it performs from day one.
